news_summary: Advances in brain health assessment, improved understanding of cholesterol testing, breakthroughs in gene-editing inspired by Dolly the sheep, and new insights into effective hydration highlight promising progress in health science today.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées en matière d'évaluation de la santé cérébrale, une meilleure compréhension des tests de cholestérol, les percées dans le domaine de l'édition génétique inspirées par la brebis Dolly, ainsi que de nouvelles perspectives sur une hydratation efficace mettent en lumière les progrès prometteurs réalisés aujourd'hui dans le domaine des sciences de la santé.
news_summary_es: Los avances en la evaluación de la salud cerebral, una mejor comprensión de los análisis de colesterol, los avances en la edición genética inspirados en la oveja Dolly y los nuevos conocimientos sobre una hidratación eficaz ponen de relieve los prometedores avances que se están produciendo actualmente en las ciencias de la salud.

articles.title: Millions may be getting the wrong cholesterol test
articles.summary: A new study suggests that apoB, a blood test that measures harmful cholesterol particles, is better than standard LDL cholesterol testing for deciding who needs more intensive treatment. Researchers found it could prevent more heart attacks and strokes while remaining cost-effective for the U.S. healthcare system.
articles.content:
Millions of Americans have a blood test every year to measure LDL, often called "bad" cholesterol. But new research from Northwestern Medicine suggests that another test may do a better job of identifying who needs more aggressive treatment to reduce the risk of heart attacks and strokes.

The study, published in JAMA, found that measuring apolipoprotein B (apoB) was more effective than tracking LDL or non HDL cholesterol when deciding whether to intensify cholesterol lowering therapy, including statins and other medications.

"We found that apoB testing to intensify cholesterol-lowering medication would prevent more heart attacks and strokes than current practice, and that these health benefits were achieved at a cost that represents good value for U.S. healthcare payers," said study lead author Ciaran Kohli-Lynch, assistant professor of preventive medicine in the division of epidemiology at Northwestern University Feinberg School of Medicine.

Kohli-Lynch said this is the first comprehensive analysis to show that using apoB to guide cholesterol treatment is also cost effective.

Heart disease remains the leading cause of death in the United States and is responsible for enormous healthcare spending. Over time, tiny cholesterol carrying particles can become trapped inside artery walls, where they build up into plaques that restrict blood flow and raise the risk of heart attacks and strokes.

Why ApoB May Be a Better Measure of Heart Disease Risk

Doctors have long relied on LDL cholesterol and non HDL cholesterol levels to decide when patients should begin or intensify cholesterol lowering treatment. While those tests provide useful information, they do not fully capture a person's cardiovascular risk.

"Research strongly shows that apolipoprotein B (apoB) is better at identifying who is at risk, because it counts the total number of harmful particles in the blood," explained Kohli-Lynch.

Unlike standard cholesterol tests, apoB measures the number of cholesterol carrying particles that can contribute to plaque buildup. Researchers say that makes it a more direct indicator of cardiovascular risk.

Even with growing evidence supporting apoB, the test is still not commonly used in routine care. Kohli-Lynch said one reason is that measuring apoB generally requires an additional blood test beyond the standard cholesterol panel, increasing both cost and inconvenience.

"Our study asked: Is it worth spending extra money to use apoB instead of LDL to guide treatment intensification?" Kohli-Lynch said.

Computer Model Compared Three Cholesterol Testing Strategies

To answer that question, the research team created a computer simulation representing 250,000 U.S. adults who were eligible for statin therapy but did not already have cardiovascular disease.

The model compared three approaches for guiding treatment:

LDL cholesterol (goal <100 mg/dL)

Non-HDL cholesterol (goal <118 mg/dL)

ApoB (goal <78.7 mg/dL)

When patients failed to meet their target, treatment was stepped up first by using stronger statins and then by adding ezetimibe if needed.

Researchers followed each strategy over a lifetime, estimating heart attacks, strokes, life expectancy, quality of life, and healthcare costs.

The results showed that using apoB to guide treatment consistently performed better than the LDL and non HDL approaches. It improved overall health outcomes, prevented more cardiovascular events, and did so in a way the researchers determined was cost effective.

New Cholesterol Guidelines Increase the Importance of Accurate Testing

The findings come as doctors have more cholesterol lowering medications available than ever before. Earlier this year, the American Heart Association and 10 other medical organizations also released updated guidelines recommending that many people begin cholesterol lowering therapy at younger ages.

"This means it is increasingly important to accurately identify who would benefit most from intensive treatment," Kohli-Lynch said.

Other Northwestern coauthors include Drs. John Wikins and Samuel Luebbe.

The study, titled "Cost-Effectiveness of ApoB, Non-HDL-C, and LDL-C Goals for Primary Prevention Lipid-Lowering Therapy," was supported by the American Heart Association Career Development Award 24CDA1274989 (Dr. Kohli-Lynch).
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant medical research breakthrough showing that apoB testing is more effective and cost-efficient than standard LDL cholesterol tests for preventing heart attacks and strokes. This has broad public health implications, potentially improving cardiovascular disease management for millions and reducing healthcare costs. The article provides detailed context, study methodology, and expert commentary, fulfilling criteria for inspiring good news with tangible benefits.
articles.personality_title: New cholesterol test may better prevent heart attacks and strokes
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Millions of Americans take blood tests each year to measure LDL cholesterol, often called “bad” cholesterol, to assess their heart disease risk. Doctors use these tests to decide if patients need stronger treatments to protect their hearts.

**What happened** – A study from Northwestern Medicine found that a different blood test, called apolipoprotein B (apoB), is better at identifying who needs more intensive cholesterol-lowering treatment. The research used a computer model comparing apoB with standard LDL and non-HDL cholesterol tests in 250,000 adults. Results showed that using apoB to guide treatment prevented more heart attacks and strokes and was cost-effective for the U.S. healthcare system.

**Impact** – ApoB measures the actual number of harmful cholesterol particles that cause artery plaque buildup, making it a clearer sign of heart risk than LDL alone. This means doctors can better decide who needs stronger medication, potentially improving health outcomes for millions. The study also showed this approach is affordable, which is important for managing healthcare costs.

**What’s next step** – With new guidelines encouraging earlier cholesterol treatment, researchers hope apoB testing will become more common in clinics. The study’s findings support changes in medical practice to include apoB testing, which could lead to better prevention of heart disease nationwide.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Measuring apoB cholesterol particles offers a smarter and cost-effective way to prevent heart attacks and strokes compared to standard cholesterol tests.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouveau test du cholestérol pourrait mieux prévenir les crises cardiaques et les AVC
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Des millions d’Américains passent chaque année un test sanguin pour mesurer le cholestérol LDL, souvent appelé « mauvais » cholestérol, afin d’évaluer leur risque cardiaque. Les médecins utilisent ces tests pour décider si un traitement plus fort est nécessaire.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une étude de Northwestern Medicine a découvert qu’un autre test sanguin, appelé apolipoprotéine B (apoB), identifie mieux les personnes ayant besoin d’un traitement plus intensif pour réduire le cholestérol. Les chercheurs ont comparé, grâce à un modèle informatique, l’apoB aux tests standards LDL et non-HDL chez 250 000 adultes. Les résultats montrent que l’apoB permet de prévenir plus d’attaques cardiaques et d’AVC, tout en restant rentable pour le système de santé américain.

**Impact** – L’apoB mesure le nombre réel de particules de cholestérol nocives qui créent des plaques dans les artères, ce qui donne un signe plus clair du risque cardiaque que le LDL seul. Cela permet aux médecins de mieux choisir qui doit recevoir un traitement plus fort, améliorant ainsi la santé de millions de personnes. L’étude montre aussi que cette méthode est abordable, ce qui est important pour maîtriser les coûts de santé.

**Prochaine étape** – Avec de nouvelles recommandations encourageant un traitement plus précoce du cholestérol, les chercheurs espèrent que le test apoB sera plus utilisé dans les cabinets médicaux. Ces résultats soutiennent un changement de pratique médicale qui pourrait mieux prévenir les maladies cardiaques à l’échelle nationale.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Mesurer les particules de cholestérol apoB est une méthode plus intelligente et rentable pour prévenir les crises cardiaques et les AVC que les tests de cholestérol habituels.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo análisis de colesterol podría prevenir mejor ataques cardíacos y accidentes cerebrovasculares
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Millones de estadounidenses se hacen cada año un análisis de sangre para medir el colesterol LDL, conocido como colesterol “malo”, para evaluar su riesgo de enfermedad cardíaca. Los médicos usan estos análisis para decidir si se necesita un tratamiento más fuerte.

**Qué pasó** – Un estudio de Northwestern Medicine encontró que otra prueba llamada apolipoproteína B (apoB) identifica mejor a las personas que necesitan un tratamiento más intenso para reducir el colesterol. Los investigadores compararon, con un modelo por computadora, la apoB con los análisis estándar de LDL y colesterol no HDL en 250,000 adultos. Los resultados mostraron que usar apoB para guiar el tratamiento previno más ataques cardíacos y accidentes cerebrovasculares y fue rentable para el sistema de salud de EE.UU.

**Impacto** – La apoB mide el número real de partículas de colesterol dañinas que forman placas en las arterias, lo que da una señal más clara del riesgo cardíaco que solo el LDL. Esto permite a los médicos decidir mejor quién necesita medicamentos más fuertes, mejorando la salud de millones. El estudio también mostró que este método es asequible, lo que ayuda a controlar los costos de salud.

**Próximo paso** – Con nuevas guías que recomiendan comenzar el tratamiento del colesterol a edades más tempranas, los investigadores esperan que la prueba apoB se use más en las clínicas. Los hallazgos apoyan cambios en la práctica médica para prevenir mejor las enfermedades cardíacas en todo el país.

**Resumen en una frase** – Medir las partículas de colesterol apoB es una manera más inteligente y rentable de prevenir ataques cardíacos y accidentes cerebrovasculares que los análisis de colesterol estándar.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a stylized human heart surrounded by gently flowing blood vessels, with glowing clusters of small, distinct particles symbolizing apolipoprotein B floating clearly within the bloodstream, contrasted against fewer, less defined cholesterol particles, all rendered in soft, natural tones of red, pink, and beige to represent improved heart health and precise medical insight.

articles.title: How healthy is your brain? We now know how to find out
articles.summary: In our efforts to keep our brains healthy, how do we know what is working? Helen Thomson explores a new generation of tests that can reveal whether our efforts are paying off
articles.content:
In our efforts to keep our brains healthy, how do we know what is working? Helen Thomson explores a new generation of tests that can reveal whether our efforts are paying off

Sam Peet

It shouldn’t have been difficult: 72 x 72. From the back seat, my daughter, newly confident in mental maths, wanted to check her answer. Whether it was because it was the end of the day, I was trying to park or something else, I stalled, cognitively speaking.

Normally, that sum wouldn’t faze me. Lately, though, I have had the sense that my brain isn’t firing on all cylinders. It’s not just maths, but a general sluggishness.

This matters to me. Not only do I write about the brain for a living, but I also work hard to keep mine healthy. I eat well, exercise and even play the trumpet, all in the hope that these things are helping.

Unlike an expanding waistline or a rise in blood pressure, however, brain health is difficult to monitor, hidden as it is behind a thick skull. But times are changing.

“I think we’re probably at the beginning of a shift toward treating brain health more like physical health – something we monitor proactively rather than reactively,” says neurologist Hedley Emsley at Lancaster University, UK.

Technological advances, artificial intelligence and an obsession with personalised health data are spawning a new generation of tools that promise to give us unrivalled insights into what’s happening between our ears, and even reveal whether your brain is in good shape for your age.

Free newsletter Sign up to Eight Weeks to a Healthier You Your science-backed guide to the easy habits that will help you sleep well, stress less, eat smarter and age better. Sign up to newsletter

So, faced with a plethora of options – from cutting-edge blood tests to expensive brain scans – I set out to discover which ones are worth it, and whether any can truly tell me if the steps I’m taking to protect my brain are working.

In the early 2000s, few people spoke about brain health. Neuroscience was more concerned with what makes a brain ill than defining what makes one thrive. Around that time, you could count the number of studies referring to brain health on both hands. Nowadays, more than 4000 studies are published on the topic each year.

Our increasing obsession with brain health

Some of that surge reflects a cultural shift. We track our steps, our sleep, our heart rate; the brain was an inevitable next step. Unease over mental health is also likely to have played a part. Around 40 per cent of US adults surveyed by Muse, a neuro-wearables company, believe they have an undiagnosed brain condition, with anxiety and depression topping the list of concerns. Given the opportunity, most people say they would take a brain health test, even if it gave information about a disease that cannot be treated or prevented.

Technology has also shifted. EEG devices that track brain activity non-invasively are cheaper than ever, and AI can now analyse, interpret and personalise brain-imaging data in real time – something that would have been impossible even five years ago. The result is a fast-growing marketplace of tools that promise to offer us insights into our brain health.

How socially connected you are is included in some definitions of brain health Pascal Maitre/Panos Pictures

Keen to get some insights about my own brain, I decide to start with something about which I already have some information: genetic testing. I had my genes analysed more than a decade ago and discovered that I possess one copy of the APOE4 gene variant, known to increase the likelihood of Alzheimer’s disease by three to four times compared with someone without it. There are plenty of tests offering to tell you whether you have this gene variant, but whether you want to do them is another question.

My own result probably makes me consider my brain health a little more than I would otherwise, but it doesn’t give me a view on how my brain is doing right now. Plus, I also know that having a family history of Alzheimer’s or a single copy of APOE4 doesn’t make the disease inevitable. In fact, Alzheimer’s organisations in the UK and US don’t recommend these tests at all, because there are so many lifestyle factors that affect one’s ultimate risk.

A more tempting option is to get a brain scan. Imaging can reveal bleeding, tumours, shrinkage, vascular damage and other age-related changes that could potentially offer valuable information about current – and future – brain health. In fact, I already have a picture of my brain stored safely in my computer from a clinical trial I took part in. It looks beautiful, and I was lucky: it didn’t pick up any unexpected anomalies known as incidental findings.

The problem is that many people aren’t so lucky, says Rab Khan, a stroke physician at the Northern Care Alliance NHS Foundation Trust in Manchester, UK. It is estimated that incidental findings are discovered in around 4 per cent of all brain scans. Some, like small cysts or unusual blood vessel growth, won’t cause harm or explain any symptoms you have, but they can lead to anxiety, further scans and expense. In fact, none of the doctors I spoke to recommends routine brain scans. “Yearly or every-few-years scanning sounds proactive, but it is not supported as a general brain health strategy,” says Khan.

With this in mind, I decide not to go for another scan, and instead look into blood tests. Simple checks of vitamin B12, folate and thyroid function can be revealing, because deficiencies and hormone imbalances can cause memory loss and cognitive impairment. My latest test, which I got as part of a “Well Woman” package, didn’t flag anything out of the ordinary, so on that basis at least, my brain seems to be doing OK.

But those tests are a fairly blunt tool and, increasingly, there are more specialised alternatives available. Alzheimer’s blood tests, for instance, can detect levels of beta-amyloid and tau, two of the proteins that contribute to the destruction of brain tissue in the condition. Some of these tests now outperform brain scans – in certain cases, detecting tell-tale signs of disease well before symptoms occur. This is exciting, but also tricky. Although such tests are available privately, they aren’t generally recommended for people without symptoms, given their predictive value is still being investigated and treatment options remain limited.

Clues in the blood

Other experimental blood tests aim to detect inflammation in the brain. Evgeniia Lobanova at the University of Cambridge and her colleagues have been studying protein clumps called specks, released by the brain’s immune cells when they spot trouble. The specks appear to drive inflammation and cell death, and their shape and size may help distinguish people who are likely to develop Parkinson’s disease or Alzheimer’s, up to five years before symptoms show. “With this test, I can look at a patient’s blood and identify those who will very much benefit from being on a drug trial,” says Lobanova.

But such tests are a way off from being available in the clinic. In the meantime, if you have serious concerns about your brain function, you can ask your family doctor for an assessment. This can flag problems with memory, concentration and awareness of time and place. There are countless online tests, too, measuring everything from reaction speed to executive function. The trouble is that all these tests are largely designed to detect impairment. What if, like me, you aren’t concerned about identifying or eliminating a specific disease, you just want to know if your brain is in good shape?

It turns out we might already be carrying the answer on our wrist. “Researchers are increasingly interested in passive behavioural signals,” says Emsley. By this, he means signals from other areas of our body that tell us something about our brain. I wear a smart watch that tracks my sleep, physical activity and heart rate to give me the lowdown on my physical health and, increasingly, researchers think these kinds of data might offer some insight into brain health, too.

Smart watch measurements while you sleep could be a shortcut to measuring brain health plainpicture/Lassalle, Bénédicte

Sleep is the obvious place to start. It is becoming clearer that sleep isn’t just restorative, but a highly organised state in which synchronised neural rhythms help drive waste clearance from the brain via the glymphatic system. In the long term, bad sleep may impair the removal of things like beta-amyloid and tau. “Many disorders that increase dementia risk also disrupt the brain’s sleep rhythms,” says Maiken Nedergaard at the University of Rochester Medicine in New York state.

So, perhaps these brain rhythms might tell us something about our overall brain health. In the past, you would have had to be wired up in a sleep lab overnight to get a glimpse at how your brainwaves are behaving, but we now have a proxy that is easier to measure. Your heart rate variability (HRV) is a measure of the tiny variations in time between heartbeats. HRV has been linked to several cognitive and mental health conditions. Low HRV is linked to a higher likelihood of depression, for instance, as well as dementia, schizophrenia and post-traumatic stress disorder.

A proxy for brain health

According to Nedergaard, a recent study by her and her colleagues may provide the critical link between heart rate dynamics and brain health. It shows that the pumps that propel glymphatic clearance are driven by the same mechanisms that drive HRV while we sleep. She speculates that HRV could be used as a biomarker for how well your brain is clearing its debris. Although the idea hasn’t yet been directly tested, the literature shows that low HRV predicts less favourable ageing, including an increased risk of dementia, she says.

I have always been a little concerned about my HRV, which is consistently lower than average, suggesting I spend a lot of time in a fight-or-flight state. I already use it as a nudge for being a little kinder to myself, doing things that help me de-stress. But knowing that it might also be a sign that my brain’s waste disposal system is being compromised helps me to further prioritise this – even if the evidence is still preliminary.

So far, though, all these signals remain fragmented. “The challenge is that the brain is far more complex and variable than something like heart rate, so I suspect the future will be less about a single ‘brain score’ and more about combining multiple signals over time to detect meaningful changes early – ideally in ways that are actionable rather than anxiety-inducing,” says Emsley.

But some researchers are taking on the challenge of developing a single test of brain health. One approach from 2018 attempts to combine four different MRI brain scans, each sensitive to separate brain problems, such as lesions, atrophy or white matter changes that reflect tissue damage, and boil this information down into one number, the brain health index (BHI).

Across several studies, David Dickie at the University of Glasgow, UK, and his colleagues showed that an individual’s BHI is tied more closely to reaction times (which are used as a proxy for healthy ageing as they reflect the brain’s ability to recognise, process and react to information) and scores on assessments of cognition than to individual tests of brain atrophy and disease alone. In 2023, a study led by Jodi Watt, also at the University of Glasgow, identified the BHI of almost 3000 people aged between 48 and 77, and derived a “normal” BHI curve for different ages. She and her colleagues later showed that people’s BHI reflected various factors that we know influence brain health. For instance, education seemed to improve it, whereas smoking, type 2 diabetes and hypertension all worsened it.

The tool still needs refining, but the appeal is obvious. Poor brain health is rarely caused by a single pathology. Combining various pieces of information into one score may be a more useful way of understanding how your brain health compares with what is typical.

But again, this seems to be focusing on what’s wrong, not what’s right. Part of the reason I want an insight into my brain health is because I try hard to improve aspects of my lifestyle that are meant to help it, so it would be nice to see those efforts rewarded, much like you see muscles grow or cholesterol drop.

In theory, it should all be making a difference. In 2024, the Lancet Commission on Dementia Prevention, Intervention and Care showed that almost half of all dementia cases could be prevented or delayed by addressing 14 lifestyle factors, including smoking, diabetes, physical activity, social isolation and hearing loss.

For Christin Glorioso, founder of NeuroAge Therapeutics, this report was particularly meaningful. A neuroscientist with a family history of Alzheimer’s and, like me, one copy of APOE4, she had a personal interest in knowing how to make her brain healthier. “Lifestyle is this huge, actionable piece that people can start working on,” she says. But she wanted some way of tracking it.

How old is your brain?

Glorioso and her colleagues had already shown that some people’s brains are biologically older or younger than their chronological age, based on patterns of gene activity in brain tissue. People whose brains were older than their chronological years were more likely to get Alzheimer’s or experience cognitive decline. Crucially, though, the researchers found that having a brain age five years younger than your actual age could offset the impact of having one copy of APOE4.

“So I thought, ‘OK, if the goal is to be minus five years, how do I track how old my brain is, and what’s working?’” says Glorioso. “And that’s how we came up with the NeuroAge test.”

At $1398 for the full package, which includes an MRI, genetic analysis, cognitive assessments and a consultation, NeuroAge is too expensive for me. But the cognitive assessment component, available for $9.99 a month, offers a rough estimate of brain age using tests of memory, attention, visuospatial performance and processing speed.

There are obvious limitations. The cognitive testing found that my brain age was 21 years younger than my real age, which I suspect reflected my knowledge of memory tricks, although Glorioso points out that my reaction times were also youthful, so maybe my brain is healthier than I think. Either way, the useful part may be less the number itself and more the chance to track changes over time. I can certainly see the appeal, although I’m not convinced I want to pay hundreds of dollars over the years to do so.

I also find it empowering to think that, even as I approach middle age, it isn’t too late to wind back the age of my brain. “Brain health isn’t just trying to stay on top of decline or disease, it’s about asking yourself ‘How can I make next year my best brain health year yet?’” says Lori Cook at the University of Texas at Dallas. “You need to recognise that potential for optimisation, that you can improve your brain health on an upward trajectory, no matter where you start.”

To help people do this, Cook and her colleagues launched the BrainHealth Project in 2020, with the aim of creating a more holistic measure of brain health that can be used to track improvements and identify what works and what doesn’t. The study aims to enrol up to 100,000 people over 10 years, collecting cognitive data through brain games, alongside lifestyle information and metrics such as HRV.

Cook also points out that brain health is about much more than just reaction times and memory. “We’re defining brain health not just as cognitive performance, but pulling from the World Health Organization’s definition of brain health, which includes social and emotional factors – how socially connected you are, how you manage stress and what activities you engage with.”

Participants provide data every six months and receive a composite score, but the point isn’t to check it against your age. “The score doesn’t define “normal” ranges because its purpose is not to compare people or groups,” says Cook. “It shows how an individual’s brain health can change and improve over time – it’s you against you.”

Multitasking is toxic for your brain

Around 40,000 people have already signed up, and early findings are beginning to show how interlinked certain factors are. Sleep appears hugely supportive for cognitive growth, for instance. Compassion seems to have a positive effect on cognition, not just wellbeing. Multitasking, by contrast, seems to have a toxic effect. “We think that constantly switching between things leads to chronic stress, which hinders neural function and impacts emotional regulation,” says Cook. So, no more mental maths while parking for me, then.

What Cook’s early results suggest, together with measures like the BHI, is that there’s no single, perfect way to assess brain health – at least not yet. However, over time, individual tests can together offer clues and patterns that reveal whether our brain health is heading in a positive or negative direction.

With that in mind, I will be keeping a watchful eye on the factors that are easiest to measure, including my sleep, HRV and social connections, and checking in every six months with the BrainHealth app. Together, they should be able to tell me if any changes I make are nudging my brain health in the right direction. And with any luck, the next time a calculation like 72 x 72 comes flying at me from the back seat, I will be in a better position to answer.
articles.reasoning: The article discusses significant advances in brain health monitoring technology and research, highlighting new tests and tools that provide actionable insights into brain health, potentially improving prevention and management of cognitive decline and dementia. It emphasizes a shift toward proactive brain health management with broad societal relevance, supported by detailed context and examples.
articles.personality_title: New tests help track and improve brain health over time
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Brain health is important but hard to measure because it happens inside our heads. Unlike checking weight or blood pressure, there hasn’t been a simple way to see if our efforts to keep our brains healthy are working.

**What happened** – Scientists and doctors have developed new tests and tools that can give clues about brain health. These include special blood tests, brain scans, and even data from smart watches that track sleep and heart rate. Researchers are also creating ways to combine different kinds of information into one score to show how healthy a brain is compared to others of the same age.

**Impact** – These advances mean people can now check their brain health more easily and track changes over time. For example, measuring heart rate changes during sleep might show how well the brain cleans itself. Brain age tests can estimate if a brain is younger or older than a person’s actual age, helping people see if lifestyle changes like exercise or better sleep are helping.

**What's next step** – Large studies are collecting brain health data from many people to better understand what helps or harms the brain. New tests will become more accurate and affordable, allowing more people to monitor their brain health regularly. This could lead to personalized advice on how to keep brains healthy longer.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New technology and tests are making it possible to track brain health more clearly, helping people see the effects of their lifestyle choices and catch problems earlier.
articles.personality_title_fr: De nouveaux tests pour suivre et améliorer la santé du cerveau
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La santé du cerveau est importante mais difficile à mesurer car elle se passe à l’intérieur de notre tête. Contrairement au poids ou à la tension artérielle, il n’existait pas de moyen simple de savoir si nos efforts pour garder notre cerveau en bonne santé fonctionnaient.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques et des médecins ont développé de nouveaux tests et outils qui donnent des indices sur la santé du cerveau. Cela comprend des analyses de sang spéciales, des scanners cérébraux, et même des données de montres connectées qui suivent le sommeil et le rythme cardiaque. Des chercheurs créent aussi des façons de combiner différentes informations en un seul score pour montrer à quel point un cerveau est sain par rapport à d’autres du même âge.

**Impact** – Ces avancées permettent maintenant aux gens de vérifier plus facilement la santé de leur cerveau et de suivre les changements dans le temps. Par exemple, mesurer les variations du rythme cardiaque pendant le sommeil peut montrer à quel point le cerveau se nettoie bien. Les tests d’âge cérébral peuvent estimer si un cerveau est plus jeune ou plus vieux que l’âge réel d’une personne, aidant ainsi à voir si des changements de mode de vie comme le sport ou un meilleur sommeil sont efficaces.

**Prochaine étape** – De grandes études collectent des données sur la santé cérébrale de nombreuses personnes pour mieux comprendre ce qui aide ou nuit au cerveau. Les nouveaux tests deviendront plus précis et abordables, permettant à plus de gens de surveiller régulièrement leur santé cérébrale. Cela pourrait conduire à des conseils personnalisés pour garder le cerveau en bonne santé plus longtemps.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La nouvelle technologie et les tests rendent possible le suivi clair de la santé du cerveau, aidant chacun à voir l’effet de ses choix de vie et à détecter les problèmes plus tôt.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevas pruebas para seguir y mejorar la salud del cerebro
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La salud del cerebro es importante pero difícil de medir porque sucede dentro de nuestra cabeza. A diferencia del peso o la presión arterial, no había una forma fácil de saber si nuestros esfuerzos para mantener el cerebro saludable estaban funcionando.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos y médicos han desarrollado nuevas pruebas y herramientas que pueden dar pistas sobre la salud del cerebro. Esto incluye análisis de sangre especiales, escáneres cerebrales e incluso datos de relojes inteligentes que registran el sueño y la frecuencia cardíaca. Los investigadores también están creando formas de combinar diferentes tipos de información en una sola puntuación para mostrar qué tan saludable está el cerebro en comparación con otros de la misma edad.

**Impacto** – Estos avances significan que ahora las personas pueden verificar su salud cerebral más fácilmente y seguir los cambios con el tiempo. Por ejemplo, medir los cambios en la frecuencia cardíaca durante el sueño puede mostrar qué tan bien se limpia el cerebro. Las pruebas de edad cerebral pueden estimar si un cerebro es más joven o más viejo que la edad real de la persona, ayudando a ver si los cambios en el estilo de vida como el ejercicio o un mejor sueño están funcionando.

**Próximo paso** – Grandes estudios están recolectando datos de salud cerebral de muchas personas para entender mejor qué ayuda o daña al cerebro. Las nuevas pruebas serán más precisas y accesibles, permitiendo que más personas monitoreen su salud cerebral regularmente. Esto podría llevar a consejos personalizados para mantener el cerebro saludable por más tiempo.

**Frase resumen** – Nuevas tecnologías y pruebas están haciendo posible seguir la salud del cerebro con claridad, ayudando a las personas a ver los efectos de sus elecciones de vida y detectar problemas antes.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing human brain composed of interconnected, gently pulsing neural pathways that resemble a network of softly lit threads, surrounded by symbolic elements like a vintage brass trumpet resting nearby, a simple smartwatch displaying a calm heart rate graph, and floating translucent DNA strands subtly weaving through the scene—all rendered in natural, muted earth tones and soft blues to evoke a sense of mindful brain health and ongoing personal care.

articles.title: From cloning to gene-editing: the enduring legacy of Dolly the sheep
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 06 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02096-1Dolly’s brush with celebrity could hold lessons for current discussions about reproductive technologies.
articles.content:
The team at the Roslin Institute was overwhelmed with media requests about Dolly.Credit: Colin McPherson/Corbis/Getty

Taxidermied, locked behind plexiglass and spinning slowly on a wooden dais in the National Museum of Scotland in Edinburgh, UK, the world’s most famous ewe remains a public spectacle three decades after her birth.

First CRISPR horses spark controversy: what’s next for gene-edited animals?

From that moment on 5 July 1996, Dolly the sheep — the first mammal cloned from an adult cell1 — was destined for celebrity, and her imprint on science is still evident in fields such as developmental biology and biotechnology. Dolly’s creation demonstrated that an adult cell can be reprogrammed to an embryonic state, which opened up the possibility of creating stem cells from adult cells. Ten years on, in 2006, details of the first such induced pluripotent stem cells were published2. The first therapies made from these cells were conditionally approved this year in Japan.

Reproductive cloning is now being used in agriculture to generate gene-edited cattle with no horns and pigs with organs that might be suitable for transplantation into humans. Moreover, an industry has sprung up to create copies of cherished pets, show animals and sport horses.

But in one important respect, Dolly’s legacy is not that anticipated in the frantic weeks after news of her birth became public in 1997. From the start, much of the world’s media portrayed Dolly as a step towards the imminent creation of cloned humans. No such development has materialized.

John Gurdon obituary: Biologist who made cloning possible

First and foremost, myriad ethical questions would surround cloning a human, not to mention formidable technical obstacles. The nuclear transfer technique that made Dolly was difficult to perform successfully in primates; monkeys would not be cloned using this method until 20183. The success rate is too low to consider the method in humans, and the risk of abnormalities resulting from the process is too high.

However, other advances in reproductive technology have been arriving at breakneck speed. Stem-cell technology, spurred by Dolly’s birth, has led to the creation of models mimicking the human embryo, and of mouse eggs and sperm from stem cells. Researchers have also developed techniques to replace faulty mitochondria in human embryos, and are engineering increasingly sophisticated artificial wombs. Last month brought a fresh wave of excitement, and worry, about the potential use of advanced gene-editing methods on embryos to create heritable genetic changes4,5.

Past imperfect

It is a lot for the public to process, particularly in the field of reproduction, in which new technologies can be portrayed as the gateway to a dystopian future and can challenge our concepts of what it means to be human. The experience of Dolly and her handlers could hold valuable lessons in how to navigate the present landscape.

Can a mouse be cloned indefinitely? Decades-long experiment has answers

The researchers who cloned Dolly, led by respected embryologist Ian Wilmut, were caught flat-footed by the frenzy of press surrounding her birth, says Bruce Whitelaw, former director of the Roslin Institute, the animal-sciences research centre near Edinburgh where she was born (and now part of the University of Edinburgh). A year earlier, the team had demonstrated that sheep could be cloned by transferring the nucleus from an embryonic cell grown in culture into an egg cell from which the nucleus had been removed6. That feat led to around 150 media requests over a week. When Dolly was announced, the institute was overwhelmed with many times this level of interest, over more than a month. “It was absolutely bonkers,” Whitelaw recalls. “We weren’t ready for that.”

The reaction to Dolly propelled genetics and reproductive biology into the public eye at a time when interactions between scientists and the media were less common than they are today, says Whitelaw. Now, ethicists, regulators and scientists are more joined up, and, increasingly, are thinking about what technologies might be next to emerge.

Ian Wilmut, embryologist who helped to produce Dolly the sheep (1944–2023)

But there is still no reliable system to prepare for the societal impact of those technologies, or to assess systematically how the public and other stakeholders might respond. Last month’s news that researchers are working out how to use a precise gene-editing technique to alter the DNA of human embryos highlights this. Some researchers say that not enough has been done to prepare the public or evaluate the ethics of heritable gene editing.

Even in the absence of such systems, governments must develop sensible regulation of new reproductive technologies. One example to emulate could be that of the UK Human Fertilisation and Embryology Authority, which proactively develops policies on fertility treatments and research involving human embryos.

At the same time, given the rapid pace of development, more countries need to adopt a structure to systematically help the public to evaluate and prepare for the possible outcomes of cutting-edge research in reproductive biology. Otherwise, confusion risks giving way to fear, and the hype that followed Dolly’s birth will inevitably be repeated.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the enduring scientific legacy of Dolly the sheep, the first mammal cloned from an adult cell, which paved the way for major advances in stem-cell research and reproductive technologies with broad implications for medicine and biotechnology. It discusses tangible progress such as induced pluripotent stem cells leading to approved therapies, gene-edited livestock for agriculture and transplantation, and ongoing ethical and regulatory considerations for gene editing in human embryos. The story has significant real-world impact on health and biomedical science, offers substantial context, and addresses the societal importance of responsibly managing emerging reproductive technologies.
articles.personality_title: Dolly the sheep's legacy shapes future of gene and reproductive research
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In 1996, Dolly the sheep became the first mammal cloned from an adult cell. This achievement showed that adult cells could be reset to an early stage, opening new paths in biology and medicine.

**What happened** – Dolly’s cloning led to important discoveries, especially in stem-cell research. Scientists learned how to create stem cells from adult cells, which later helped develop new medical treatments. Today, cloning and gene editing are used to produce animals like hornless cattle and pigs with organs that might help human patients.

**Impact** – Dolly’s story changed how scientists understand development and genetics. It also pushed public attention and discussions about cloning and gene editing. Although cloning humans has not happened due to ethical and technical challenges, Dolly’s case helped prepare society for new reproductive technologies now being explored.

**What’s next step** – Researchers continue to improve gene-editing methods, including editing human embryos to prevent diseases. Governments and scientists are working on rules and ways to talk with the public about these advances to avoid fear and confusion.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Dolly the sheep’s cloning sparked scientific breakthroughs that continue to influence medicine and guide how society approaches new gene and reproductive technologies.
articles.personality_title_fr: L'héritage de Dolly la brebis façonne l'avenir de la recherche génétique et reproductive
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – En 1996, Dolly la brebis est devenue le premier mammifère cloné à partir d'une cellule adulte. Cette réussite a montré que les cellules adultes pouvaient être ramenées à un stade précoce, ouvrant de nouvelles voies en biologie et en médecine.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le clonage de Dolly a conduit à des découvertes importantes, notamment dans la recherche sur les cellules souches. Les scientifiques ont appris à créer des cellules souches à partir de cellules adultes, ce qui a aidé au développement de nouveaux traitements médicaux. Aujourd’hui, le clonage et l’édition génétique sont utilisés pour produire des animaux comme des bovins sans cornes et des cochons dont les organes pourraient aider les patients humains.

**Impact** – L’histoire de Dolly a changé la compréhension scientifique du développement et de la génétique. Elle a aussi attiré l’attention du public sur le clonage et l’édition génétique. Bien que le clonage humain n’ait pas eu lieu à cause de défis éthiques et techniques, le cas de Dolly a aidé à préparer la société aux nouvelles technologies reproductives explorées aujourd’hui.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs continuent d’améliorer les méthodes d’édition génétique, y compris sur les embryons humains pour prévenir des maladies. Les gouvernements et scientifiques travaillent à des règles et à des dialogues avec le public pour éviter peur et confusion.

**Message clé en une phrase** – Le clonage de Dolly la brebis a déclenché des avancées scientifiques qui influencent encore la médecine et guident la société face aux nouvelles technologies génétiques et reproductives.
articles.personality_title_es: El legado de Dolly la oveja moldea el futuro de la investigación genética y reproductiva
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En 1996, Dolly la oveja se convirtió en el primer mamífero clonado a partir de una célula adulta. Este logro mostró que las células adultas pueden ser reiniciadas a una etapa temprana, abriendo nuevos caminos en biología y medicina.

**Qué pasó** – La clonación de Dolly llevó a importantes descubrimientos, especialmente en la investigación con células madre. Los científicos aprendieron a crear células madre a partir de células adultas, lo que ayudó a desarrollar nuevos tratamientos médicos. Hoy, la clonación y edición genética se usan para producir animales como ganado sin cuernos y cerdos con órganos que podrían ayudar a pacientes humanos.

**Impacto** – La historia de Dolly cambió la forma en que los científicos entienden el desarrollo y la genética. También llamó la atención pública sobre la clonación y edición genética. Aunque no se ha clonado humanos debido a desafíos éticos y técnicos, el caso de Dolly ayudó a preparar a la sociedad para nuevas tecnologías reproductivas.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores siguen mejorando técnicas de edición genética, incluyendo la edición de embriones humanos para prevenir enfermedades. Gobiernos y científicos trabajan en reglas y en dialogar con el público para evitar miedo y confusión.

**Resumen en una frase** – La clonación de Dolly la oveja impulsó avances científicos que siguen influyendo en la medicina y guían a la sociedad frente a nuevas tecnologías genéticas y reproductivas.
articles.image_prompt: A softly lit, detailed painting of a gentle sheep standing calmly inside a transparent, gently glowing glass orb symbolizing scientific discovery, surrounded by swirling, delicate strands of DNA and softly glowing embryonic cells, all rendered in warm, natural earth tones and muted pastels.

articles.title: Time to give hydration breaks the red card? What science says about keeping cool
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 06 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02097-0Inappropriate use of cooling breaks in sport is undermining trust in heat-health research.
articles.content:
Curaçao’s team physician hands out ice-pack jackets to help players cool down during a men’s football World Cup 2026 match in Kansas City, Missouri.Credit: Robbie Jay Barratt/AMA/Getty

Twice each game, the World Cup 2026 hits pause. Drinks appear, adverts go up and fans boo. The football governing body FIFA says that these ‘hydration breaks’ are about player welfare: a response to rising temperatures and the growing risk of heat exhaustion. But right now, FIFA is missing the mark, and risks turning a sound idea into a poor example.

To save lives in heatwaves, focus on how human bodies work

I say this as a researcher who investigates the effect of heat on health and performance. Cooling breaks can work. The science is strong. But they must be executed properly.

Put simply: cooling breaks should be driven by heat-stress risk and designed around effective cooling, not broadcast schedules or commercial pressures.

On the pitch, that principle is starting to slip. Breaks that should be calibrated to environmental heat stress seem to be tied to television schedules and advertising incomes. Critics complain that the time meant for cooling is used for tactical instructions, and players are receiving guidance while standing in the sun rather than in the shade.

FIFA’s medical guidelines are clear: cooling breaks are intended solely to prevent heat illness and should be used when environmental heat stress exceeds a defined threshold (a wet-bulb globe temperature of 32 °C).

But in this World Cup, there’s a gap between policy and practice. Breaks appear in every match, regardless of conditions, even in climate-controlled stadiums. Although it might seem fair to treat all games in the same way, this blanket approach risks undermining trust in heat-safety measures. If breaks are always used, regardless of risk, they stop being meaningful and start looking like routine stoppages.

Repeated heatwaves can age you as much as smoking or drinking

Heat exhaustion in athletes is common, but preventable. My collaborators and I have run controlled experiments with participants simulating 90-minute football matches in 40 °C heat and 41% humidity. We tested different cooling approaches: passive breaks, active cooling and longer recovery periods. The difference was stark. Short in-game breaks using ice-soaked towels and cold drinks, combined with a slightly longer half-time pause, reduced core temperature and cardiovascular strain considerably. By contrast, breaks without active cooling offered little measurable benefit (H. A. Brown et al. J. Sci. Med. Sport 28, 491–497 (2025); H. A. Brown et al. Br. J. Sports Med. 58, 1044–1051; 2024).

It’s not the pause in play — it’s how it’s used. If players aren’t actively cooling, heat strain remains a problem.

This isn’t just a problem for sport. It’s an issue for human health. The same principles apply to construction workers on a hot building site or agricultural workers in a field. Across these settings, the evidence is consistent: structured interventions that combine rest, hydration and active cooling reduce physiological strain and protect health. When they’re poorly designed or inconsistently applied, the benefits might not be felt.

That wider relevance is why getting this right in football matters. Global sport sets the tone. When governing bodies misapply science in highly visible events, it risks normalizing ineffective approaches. But there are positive examples of heat-management strategies. Some sports have moved beyond temperature thresholds to physiology-first, responsive management of heat stress. The point here is flexibility, guided by science.

What is the hottest temperature humans can survive? These labs are redefining the limit

In tennis, the Australian Open, for example, uses a five-level heat-stress scale, which was developed with researchers from my centre. It combines physiological risk of heat stress with environmental measurements, and the response is adjusted accordingly. That can mean anything from implementing cooling measures courtside to suspending play. Since 2025, World Rugby has taken a similar approach, working with our centre to introduce evidence-based heat guidelines that tailor interventions to both conditions and individual player risk.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights scientifically backed strategies to prevent heat exhaustion in athletes and workers by implementing effective cooling breaks. It emphasizes the importance of evidence-based heat management policies in sports and other labor-intensive fields, which can reduce health risks associated with heat stress on a broad scale. This represents a meaningful public health advancement with potential large-scale impact.
articles.personality_title: Science shows how to make cooling breaks work better in sports
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In the 2026 World Cup, FIFA introduced hydration breaks to help players cool down during games played in hot conditions. These breaks aim to prevent heat exhaustion, a serious health risk when temperatures rise.

**What happened** – Researchers found that while cooling breaks are a good idea, they are not always used correctly. Instead of being based on real heat danger, breaks are sometimes scheduled to fit TV commercials or game plans. This means players might stand in the sun without proper cooling, reducing the breaks’ benefit.

**Impact** – Proper cooling breaks that include ice packs and cold drinks can lower players’ body temperatures and protect their hearts. This approach also applies to workers in hot jobs like construction and farming, showing the wider importance of using science to manage heat safely. When big sports events get this right, it sets a good example for others.

**What's next step** – Some sports, like tennis and rugby, are already using more detailed heat-risk systems that adjust breaks and cooling methods based on real conditions and player needs. FIFA and others could follow these models to improve heat safety in future games.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Cooling breaks can prevent heat illness if they are based on real heat risks and include active cooling, not just scheduled pauses during games.
articles.personality_title_fr: La science montre comment améliorer les pauses rafraîchissantes dans le sport
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Lors de la Coupe du Monde 2026, la FIFA a introduit des pauses hydratation pour aider les joueurs à se rafraîchir pendant les matchs joués par temps chaud. Ces pauses visent à prévenir l'épuisement dû à la chaleur, un risque sérieux pour la santé quand les températures montent.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs ont découvert que, bien que les pauses rafraîchissantes soient une bonne idée, elles ne sont pas toujours bien utilisées. Au lieu d'être basées sur un vrai danger lié à la chaleur, les pauses sont parfois programmées pour les publicités à la télévision ou les tactiques de jeu. Cela signifie que les joueurs peuvent rester au soleil sans refroidissement approprié, ce qui réduit l'efficacité des pauses.

**Impact** – Des pauses correctement utilisées, avec des packs de glace et des boissons froides, peuvent faire baisser la température corporelle des joueurs et protéger leur cœur. Cette méthode s'applique aussi aux travailleurs exposés à la chaleur, comme dans la construction ou l'agriculture, montrant l'importance plus large d'une gestion scientifique de la chaleur. Quand les grands événements sportifs font bien cela, ils donnent un bon exemple.

**Prochaine étape** – Certains sports, comme le tennis et le rugby, utilisent déjà des systèmes plus précis d'évaluation des risques de chaleur qui adaptent les pauses et les méthodes de refroidissement selon les conditions réelles et les besoins des joueurs. La FIFA et d'autres pourraient suivre ces modèles pour améliorer la sécurité contre la chaleur dans les futurs matchs.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Les pauses rafraîchissantes peuvent prévenir les maladies liées à la chaleur si elles sont basées sur de vrais risques et comprennent un refroidissement actif, pas seulement des arrêts programmés pendant les matchs.
articles.personality_title_es: La ciencia muestra cómo mejorar las pausas para refrescarse en el deporte
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En la Copa Mundial 2026, la FIFA introdujo pausas para hidratación que ayudan a los jugadores a refrescarse durante partidos en condiciones de calor. Estas pausas buscan prevenir el agotamiento por calor, un riesgo serio para la salud cuando suben las temperaturas.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores encontraron que, aunque las pausas para refrescarse son una buena idea, no siempre se usan correctamente. En lugar de basarse en el riesgo real por calor, a veces se programan para encajar con comerciales de televisión o estrategias del juego. Esto hace que los jugadores estén bajo el sol sin un enfriamiento adecuado, reduciendo el beneficio de las pausas.

**Impacto** – Pausas bien usadas, con bolsas de hielo y bebidas frías, pueden bajar la temperatura corporal de los jugadores y proteger su corazón. Esta manera también sirve para trabajadores en trabajos calurosos como construcción o agricultura, mostrando la importancia de usar la ciencia para manejar el calor de forma segura. Cuando los grandes eventos deportivos lo hacen bien, marcan un buen ejemplo.

**Próximo paso** – Algunos deportes, como el tenis y el rugby, ya usan sistemas más detallados de riesgo por calor que ajustan las pausas y métodos de enfriamiento según las condiciones reales y las necesidades de los jugadores. FIFA y otros podrían seguir estos modelos para mejorar la seguridad contra el calor en futuros juegos.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Las pausas para refrescarse pueden prevenir enfermedades por calor si se basan en riesgos reales y incluyen enfriamiento activo, no solo paradas programadas durante los partidos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a vibrant football pitch under a warm sun, featuring symbolic ice-pack jackets draped over goalposts and stylized water droplets gently cooling abstract, dynamic player silhouettes in mid-action, all rendered in soft, natural earth tones and cool blues to evoke active cooling and heat relief.
